text: mlmodels estimating functions allow explicitly control whether intercept included value (mean) equation /scale equation. done dedicated arguments noint_value noint_scale. Trying formulas value /scale arguments throw error abort estimation. Let’s see : code catch error reproduce stop message received hardhat::mold(), rest code executed, can see wording receive try . can see second estimation’s results intercept estimate value equation, wanted. Important Notes Using formula syntax like ~0 + ... ~-1 + ... work expected. trigger errors hardhat::mold(). noint_value = TRUE → removes intercept mean/value equation noint_scale = TRUE → removes intercept scale equation (present) ml_poisson() noint_value (proability doesn’t scale parameter). design? models use hardhat blueprints consistent safe formula handling. noint_* arguments give explicit, reliable control intercepts maintaining compatibility internal structure.
code: # No intercept in the value function (wrong way) fit_no_int <- tryCatch({   ml_lm(incthou ~ 0 + age + I(age^2) + huswage + educ + unem,                  scale = ~ educ + exper,                   data = mroz) }, error = function(e) {   print(e)   invisible(NULL) }) #> <error/rlang_error> #> Error in `hardhat::mold()`: #> ! `formula` must not contain the intercept removal term: `+ 0` or `0 +`. #> --- #> Backtrace: #>     ▆ #>  1. ├─base::tryCatch(...) #>  2. │ └─base (local) tryCatchList(expr, classes, parentenv, handlers) #>  3. │   └─base (local) tryCatchOne(expr, names, parentenv, handlers[[1L]]) #>  4. │     └─base (local) doTryCatch(return(expr), name, parentenv, handler) #>  5. └─mlmodels::ml_lm(...) #>  6.   ├─hardhat::mold(value, data_clean, blueprint = hardhat::default_formula_blueprint(intercept = !noint_value)) #>  7.   └─hardhat:::mold.formula(value, data_clean, blueprint = hardhat::default_formula_blueprint(intercept = !noint_value))  # No intercept in the value function (right way) fit_no_int <- ml_lm(incthou ~ age + I(age^2) + huswage + educ + unem,                  scale = ~ educ + exper,                   noint_value = TRUE,                  data = mroz) summary(fit_no_int) #>  #> Maximum Likelihood Model #>  Type: Heteroskedastic Linear Model  #> --------------------------------------- #> Call: #> ml_lm(value = incthou ~ age + I(age^2) + huswage + educ + unem,  #>     scale = ~educ + exper, data = mroz, noint_value = TRUE) #>  #> Log-Likelihood: -2627.74  #>  #> Wald significance tests: #>  all: Chisq(7) = 6994.167, Pr(>Chisq) = < 1e-8 #>  Mean: Chisq(5) = 6919.276, Pr(>Chisq) = < 1e-8 #>  Scale: Chisq(2) = 33.037, Pr(>Chisq) = 0.0000 #>  #> Variance type: Original Information Matrix #> --------------------------------------- #>                       Estimate Std. title: Introduction to Count Data
text: One key concerns Poisson model overdispersion. Cameron Trivedi present regression-based test overdispersion (NB2 parameterization) provide code implement . mlmodels implemented test convenient function compares Poisson model NB1 NB2 forms overdispersion: output shows strong evidence overdispersion specifications (highly significant t-statistics NB1 NB2). confirms two important points: interested inference mean parameters, using robust standard errors Poisson model usually sufficient. However, also care correctly modeling variance making accurate probability predictions (e.g., P(Y=0), P(Y=k), etc.), Poisson model inadequate. cases, moving negative binomial model appropriate.

title: Diagnostic Tools in `mlmodels`
text: maximum likelihood estimators, main question usually ask : information matrix equality hold? information matrix test – White (1982) – attempts answer question. Even though test misspecification, practitioner’s point view, test inference model. test rejects equality, inference done using robust methods. mlmodels IMtest() function offers four methods test: quad – Analyitical quadratic orthogonalized (Davidson MacKinnon, 1992). boot_quad – Analyitical quadratic orthogonalized plus bootstrapped p-values. opg – Analytical Chesher/Lancester score version test (Chesher, 1983; Lancaster, 1984). boot_opg – Analytical Chesher/Lancaster score version test plus bootstrapped p-values. Even though analytical versions test asymptotically equivalent, shown practical limitations. Chesher/Lancaster (OPG) version known extreme size distortion, often rejecting correctly specified models (Horowitz, 1994). orthogonalized quadratic version robust size problems, construction can lead loss power, failing detect actual misspecification. Bootstrapping tests – approach pioneered IM test Horowitz (1994) championed Davidson MacKinnon (1999) – bridges gaps. Rather relying asymptotic critical values, implementation provides bootstrap p-values estimating test statistic’s distribution directly sample data, significantly improves reliability test finite samples.

title: Fractional Response Outcomes
text: Fractional response variables – outcomes take values unit interval [0, 1] – common applied research. Examples include employment rates, participation rates pension plans, debt--asset ratios, market shares. Modeling variables correctly challenging bounded often exhibit heteroskedasticity depends conditional mean. Ordinary least squares (OLS) invalid setting. predicted values constrained unit interval, assumption constant variance clearly violated. Moreover, linear functional form typically misspecified bounded dependent variable. Papke Wooldridge (1996), common practice apply log-odds transformation, log(y/(1-y)), estimate model OLS. approach two serious drawbacks. First, undefined outcome takes values 0 1. Second, even can computed, recovering predicted values marginal effects original scale y requires complex nonlinear re-transformation parameters. boundary values present, researchers sometimes turned Berkson’s minimum chi-square estimator. However, method valid observed proportions continuous (derived continuous underlying variables), typical case modern microeconomic data. limitations motivated search better estimators. Papke Wooldridge (1996) showed quasi-maximum likelihood estimation (QMLE) based logistic (logit) standard normal (probit) cumulative distribution function provides consistent estimates conditional mean parameters, even though data fractional Bernoulli variance assumption generally incorrect. information matrix identity hold, robust (sandwich) standard errors required valid inference. outcome strictly fractional (observations 0 1), Beta regression model becomes attractive alternative. provides full maximum likelihood estimator assumption y follows Beta distribution, often efficient quasi-likelihood approach. However, like log-odds transformation, Beta model undefined boundaries automatically drops observations y = 0 y = 1. potential gain efficiency comes cost: Beta model robust distributional misspecification. Beta distributional assumption fails, estimator can easily become inconsistent parameters interest. vignette compares three approaches — logit probit QMLE (can handle boundary values) Beta MLE () — using classic 401(k) participation data Papke Wooldridge (1996).

However, implicit Bernoulli variance assumption usually incorrect fractional data, information matrix identity hold. Consequently, standard errors based observed information matrix (oim) outer product gradients (opg) generally invalid. Robust (sandwich) standard errors essential reliable inference. mlmodels package supports fractional responses ml_logit() ml_probit(). addition, automatically warn users attempt post-estimation inference using oim opg standard errors fractional outcomes. detailed discussion different variance-covariance estimators available, see vignette Variance-Covariance Estimation mlmodels.

title: Predictions with `mlmodels`
text: predict() function mlmodels returns object class predict.mlmodel, list composed two elements: fit: predicted values. se.fit: standard errors calculated via delta method using analytical gradients (present se.fit = TRUE). default, predict() returns -sample predictions. automatically aligns results original dataset, inserting NA observations dropped estimation (due missing values, subsetting, invalid outcome values specific model, non-positive values gamma lognormal models, values outside (0,1) beta regression).

title: Predictions with `mlmodels`
code: data(mroz) mroz$incthou <- mroz$faminc / 1000  fit <- ml_lm(incthou ~ age + I(age^2) + educ + kidslt6, data = mroz)  # Default: expected value (response) pred <- predict(fit) head(pred$fit) #> [1] 19.74355 18.70671 21.28132 20.98756 23.15382 23.17217  # With standard errors pred_se <- predict(fit, se.fit = TRUE) head(data.frame(fit = pred_se$fit, se = pred_se$se.fit)) #>        fit        se #> 1 19.74355 0.8506750 #> 2 18.70671 1.2325354 #> 3 21.28132 0.7538523 #> 4 20.98756 0.7605700 #> 5 23.15382 0.9533716 #> 6 23.17217 0.7866816

title: Predictions with `mlmodels`
text: two main approaches bootstrap-based inference: Bootstrapped variance + delta method - compute bootstrapped variance-covariance matrix, use calculate standard errors confidence intervals (similar coefficients estimation). Full bootstrap quantity - refit model many bootstrap samples, compute prediction (marginal effect) sample’s estimation, derive confidence intervals percentiles distribution. marginaleffects uses second approach (full bootstrap predictions) specify vcov = "boot" predicting functions, use inferences(method = "boot"). method computationally heavier, produces robust confidence intervals. Since mlmodels also uses string "boot" option get bootstrapped variance, way obtain bootstrapped standard errors (approach 1) marginaleffects, pre-compute bootstrapped variance-covariance matrix vcov(), pass predictions(). example confidence intervals methods similar. general, however, full bootstrap approach (bootstrapping predictions ) robust distributional assumptions model may hold perfectly. extra robustness comes cost higher computational time compared using bootstrapped variance matrix delta method. marginaleffects gives flexibility choose whichever approach best suits needs computational budget.
code: ## 1st approach (Bootstrapped variance) # pre-compute the variance on the linear model (low number of repetitions to make it fast) v_boot <- vcov(fit, type = "boot", repetitions = 200, seed = 123, progress = FALSE) # use it with predictions() boot_delta <- predictions(fit, vcov = v_boot)  ## 2nd approach (Bootstrapped prediction) boot_pred <- predictions(fit) |>   inferences(method = "boot", R = 200) # Inferences allows you to set the repetitions.  # The estimate is the same in both methods comp <- data.frame(   obs = 1:8,   Estimate = boot_delta$estimate[1:8],   Delta_Low = boot_delta$conf.low[1:8],   Delta_High = boot_delta$conf.high[1:8],   Pred_Low = boot_pred$conf.low[1:8],   Pred_High = boot_pred$conf.high[1:8] )  comp #>   obs Estimate Delta_Low Delta_High Pred_Low Pred_High #> 1   1 19.74355  17.74283   21.74428 17.68970  22.15990 #> 2   2 18.70671  16.80978   20.60364 16.94558  20.72125 #> 3   3 21.28132  19.35007   23.21257 19.26963  23.44030 #> 4   4 20.98756  19.79893   22.17619 19.74013  22.31944 #> 5   5 23.15382  20.90551   25.40214 21.10759  25.54654 #> 6   6 23.17217  21.69891   24.64542 21.69350  24.67788 #> 7   7 30.29423  28.14842   32.44003 28.38837  32.41074 #> 8   8 23.17217  21.69891   24.64542 21.69350  24.67788

title: Variance-Covariance Estimation in `mlmodels`
text: Accurate variance-covariance estimation essential statistical inference maximum likelihood models. variance-covariance matrix allows us : Compute standard errors parameter estimates Construct confidence intervals Perform hypothesis tests (Wald, z-tests, etc.) Calculate p-values assess statistical significance Without reliable variance-covariance matrix, make valid inferences parameters model. mlmodels package provides comprehensive set variance-covariance estimators, vcov() implementation, ranging classical information matrix methods robust resampling-based approaches. vignette explains method, use , relate .

title: Variance-Covariance Estimation in `mlmodels`
text: Different estimators make different assumptions model: Classical methods (oim, opg) assume model correctly specified (Information Matrix Identity holds). Robust methods relax assumption valid general conditions. Resampling methods (bootstrap, jackknife) make even fewer parametric assumptions often robust, cost higher computation time. Choosing right variance estimator therefore important part responsible statistical practice.

title: Variance-Covariance Estimation in `mlmodels`
text: alternative classical estimator Outer Product Gradients (also known BHHH). maximum likelihood estimation model well specified, negative Hessian matrix (base oim variance), outer product gradients matrix (basis opg variance) whould equal. called Information Matrix Identity. maximum likelihood model correctly specified, Information Matrix Identity holds. means negative expected Hessian matrix (basis oimvariance) equal (expectation) outer product gradients matrix (basis opg variance). practice, can compare two estimators get sense model adequacy: model well identified correctly specified, oim opg standard errors similar. Significant differences can indication model misspecification weak identification. one reasons mlmodels provides robust variance estimators (robust, boot, jack) — rely Information Matrix Identity holding.

title: Variance-Covariance Estimation in `mlmodels`
text: computing oim variance, may occasionally see following warning: ! OIM variance may unreliable due singularity Hessian. warning indicates observed information matrix close singular (nearly non-invertible). means: strong diagnostic signal model misspecification numerical instability. Common causes include: Multicollinearity among predictors. Overparameterized models. Inappropriate specification value scale equations. Poor identification parameters. mean: automatically mean coefficient estimates meaningless. imply misspecified models trigger warning. likely misspecified.

text: package also provides two resampling-based variance estimators: boot - Bootstrap (optional clustering). jack/jacknife - Jackknife (optional clustering). title: Information Matrix Test for Model Misspecification — IMtest
text: object fitted model object inheriting "mlmodel". ... arguments passed methods (currently used). method Character string. Specifies version test: "quad" (default): Quadratic form Information Matrix test (common). "opg": Outer Product Gradients version (Chesher-Lancaster). "boot_quad": Analytical chi-square p-value, plus bootstrap p-value quadratic form. "boot_opg": Analytical chi-square p-value, plus bootstrap p-value OPG version. repetitions Integer. Number bootstrap replications using bootstrap method. Default 999. seed Integer. Random seed reproducibility bootstrap methods. NULL, random seed generated.

title: Information Matrix Test for Model Misspecification — IMtest
text: object class "IMtest.mlmodel" containing analytical test statistic, degrees freedom p-value, plus bootstrapped p-value (bootstrap method selected).

title: Information Matrix Test for Model Misspecification — IMtest
text: Information Matrix test checks whether model correctly specified testing equality Hessian outer product gradient (information matrix equality). Rejection null hypothesis indicates model misspecification (e.g., incorrect functional form, heteroskedasticity properly modeled, omitted variables, etc.). Two main versions implemented: Quadratic form ("quad"): Generally preferred better finite-sample properties. OPG version ("opg"): Chesher Lancaster (1983) version. Bootstrap versions ("boot_quad" "boot_opg") provide p-values based empirical distribution test statistic useful asymptotic approximations may unreliable.

title: Information Matrix Test for Model Misspecification — IMtest
text: Chesher, . (1983). information matrix test: Simplified calculation via score test interpretation. Economics Letters, 13(1), 45-48. Lancaster, T. (1984). covariance matrix information matrix test. Econometrica, 52(4), 1051-1053. White, H. (1982). Maximum likelihood estimation misspecified models. Econometrica, 50(1), 1-25.

title: Information Matrix Test for Model Misspecification — IMtest
text: Alfonso Sanchez-Penalver

title: Information Matrix Test for Model Misspecification — IMtest
code: # Linear model example data(mroz) mroz$incthou <- mroz$faminc / 1000  fit <- ml_lm(incthou ~ age + I(age^2) + huswage + educ + unem,               data = mroz)  # Default quadratic form test IMtest(fit) #> Information Matrix Test #>  Method: Orthogonalized Quadratic Form  #>  Model:  Homoskedastic Linear Model  #> -------------------------------------------- #>  Chisq(28) = 122.732    Pr(>Chisq) = 0.0000 #> --------------------------------------------  # OPG version IMtest(fit, method = "opg") #> Information Matrix Test #>  Method: Chesher/Lancaster OPG  #>  Model:  Homoskedastic Linear Model  #> -------------------------------------------- #>  Chisq(28) = 298.563    Pr(>Chisq) = 0.0000 #> --------------------------------------------  # Bootstrap p-value (quadratic form) IMtest(fit, method = "boot_quad", repetitions = 100, seed = 123) #> Information Matrix Test #>  Method: Orthogonalized Quadratic Form + Model-based bootstrap  #>  Model:  Homoskedastic Linear Model  #> -------------------------------------------- #>  Repetitions: Total 100 Successful 100  #>  Chisq(28) = 122.732 #>  P(>Chisq): Analytical   = 0.0000  #>             Bootstrapped = 0.7700 #> --------------------------------------------  # Heteroskedastic model fit_het <- ml_lm(incthou ~ age + I(age^2) + huswage + educ + unem,                  scale = ~ educ, data = mroz) IMtest(fit_het) #> Information Matrix Test #>  Method: Orthogonalized Quadratic Form  #>  Model:  Heteroskedastic Linear Model  #> -------------------------------------------- #>  Chisq(36) = 163.110    Pr(>Chisq) = 0.0000 #> --------------------------------------------

title: Overdispersion Tests for Count Models — OVDtest
text: Alfonso Sanchez-Penalver

title: Overdispersion Tests for Count Models — OVDtest
code: # Poisson model fit_pois <- ml_poisson(docvis ~ private + medicaid + age + I(age^2) +                         educyr + actlim + totchr, data = docvis) OVDtest(fit_pois) #>  #> Cameron and Trivedi (1990) Overdispersion Test: #> -------------------------------------- #>   H0: Poisson (alpha = 0) #>   H1: Overdispersion (alpha > 0) #> -------------------------------------- #>     Estimate t-stat p-value     #> NB2   0.7047 6.8029  0.0000 *** #> NB1   5.3043 6.8383  0.0000 *** #> --- #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #> Observations: 3677  #> Note: P-values are based on a one-tailed t-test (Right Tail). #>   # Negative binomial model (the test still fits a Poisson internally using  # only the value equation, so results are identical) fit_nb2 <- ml_negbin(docvis ~ private + medicaid + age + I(age^2) +                       educyr + actlim + totchr, data = docvis) OVDtest(fit_nb2) #>  #> Cameron and Trivedi (1990) Overdispersion Test: #> -------------------------------------- #>   H0: Poisson (alpha = 0) #>   H1: Overdispersion (alpha > 0) #> -------------------------------------- #>     Estimate t-stat p-value     #> NB2   0.7047 6.8029  0.0000 *** #> NB1   5.3043 6.8383  0.0000 *** #> --- #> Signif. codes: 0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1 #> Observations: 3677  #> Note: P-values are based on a one-tailed t-test (Right Tail). #>

text: object_1 fitted model object inheriting "mlmodel". Typically restricted (smaller) model. object_2 fitted model object inheriting "mlmodel". Typically unrestricted (larger) model. order object_1 object_2 matter — function automatically determines restricted model. ... arguments passed methods (currently used).

title: Likelihood Ratio Test for Nested mlmodel Objects — lrtest
text: object class "lrtest.mlmodel" test statistic, degrees freedom, p-value.

title: Likelihood Ratio Test for Nested mlmodel Objects — lrtest
text: likelihood ratio test statistic calculated : $$LR = 2 \times (\log L_{\text{unrestricted}} - \log L_{\text{restricted}})$$ null hypothesis restricted model correct, LR follows \(\chi^2\) distribution degrees freedom equal difference number parameters two models. Important: two models must nested (restricted model must special case unrestricted one) fitted exactly sample. restricted model must lower (equal) log-likelihood.

title: Likelihood Ratio Test for Nested mlmodel Objects — lrtest
text: Alfonso Sanchez-Penalver

title: Likelihood Ratio Test for Nested mlmodel Objects — lrtest
code: # Linear model example data(mroz) mroz$incthou <- mroz$faminc / 1000  fit_small <- ml_lm(incthou ~ age + huswage, data = mroz) fit_large <- ml_lm(incthou ~ age + I(age^2) + huswage + educ + unem,                     data = mroz)  lrtest(fit_small, fit_large) #> ℹ `object_1` is the restricted model (nested in `object_2`). #> Likelihood Ratio Test #> -------------------------------------------- #> Chisq(3) = 61.194    Pr(>Chisq) = < 1e-8 #> -------------------------------------------- #> LogLik (restricted)   : -2669.275   (df = 4) #> LogLik (unrestricted) : -2638.678   (df = 7)  # You can also reverse the order — the function detects the restricted model lrtest(fit_large, fit_small) #> ℹ `object_2` is the restricted model (nested in `object_1`). #> Likelihood Ratio Test #> -------------------------------------------- #> Chisq(3) = 61.194    Pr(>Chisq) = < 1e-8 #> -------------------------------------------- #> LogLik (restricted)   : -2669.275   (df = 7) #> LogLik (unrestricted) : -2638.678   (df = 4)

what: mlmodels: Maximum Likelihood Models and Tools for Estimation, Prediction, and Testing — mlmodels-package
title: mlmodels: Maximum Likelihood Models and Tools for Estimation, Prediction, and Testing — mlmodels-package
text: Provides collection maximum likelihood estimators consistent S3 interface. Supported models include Gaussian (linear log-normal), logit, probit, Poisson, negative binomial (NB1 NB2), gamma, beta regression. distinctive feature flexible modeling scale parameter (variance, dispersion, precision, shape) alongside location/mean parameters. package offers unified predict() methods, multiple variance-covariance estimators (observed information, outer product gradients, robust/Huber-White, cluster-robust, bootstrap, jackknife), full suite hypothesis tests (Wald, likelihood ratio, information matrix). fully compatible marginaleffects post-estimation analysis.

code: # Basic usage and different predict types data(docvis) fit_pois <- ml_poisson(docvis ~ age + educyr + totchr, data = docvis)  head(predict(fit_pois, type = "response")$fit)     # Expected count #> [1] 10.169550  6.266430  6.795981  9.255944  5.471075  5.002632 head(predict(fit_pois, type = "P(3)")$fit)         # Prob of exactly 3 #> [1] 0.006716988 0.077881334 0.058498950 0.012627153 0.114817735 0.140226107  # Prediction at the mean (typical case) typical <- data.frame(age = mean(docvis$age),                        educyr = mean(docvis$educyr),                        totchr = mean(docvis$totchr)) predict(fit_pois, newdata = typical, type = "response") #> $fit #> [1] 6.31286 #>  #> $se.fit #> NULL #>  #> attr(,"class") #> [1] "predict.ml_poisson" "predict.mlmodel"     # In-sample vs full-data prediction with subset / boundary dropping data(pw401k) fit_beta <- ml_beta(prate ~ mrate + I(mrate^2) + log(totemp) +                      I(log(totemp)^2) + age + I(age^2) + sole,                     data = pw401k,                      subset = prate < 1) #> ℹ Improving initial values by scaling (factor = 0.5). #> ℹ Initial log-likelihood: -311.974 #> ℹ Final scaled log-likelihood: 79.945  # In-sample prediction (NAs for dropped observations) head(predict(fit_beta, type = "response")$fit) #> [1] 0.8034086 0.7705266        NA 0.7500891 0.7293741        NA  # Full-data prediction (predicts for all rows, including dropped ones) head(predict(fit_beta, newdata = pw401k, type = "response")$fit) #> [1] 0.8034086 0.7705266 0.8783125 0.7500891 0.7293741 0.8903587

title: Update an mlmodel Call — update.mlmodel
text: method re-evaluates original model call modifying selected arguments. used internally IMtest() serves fallback mechanism bootstrap jackknife variance estimation model-specific implementation available. sandwich package compatibility functions sandwich::vcovBS() sandwich::vcovJK() supported update() method. produce numerically equivalent results vcov(object, type = "boot") vcov(object, type = "jack") bootstrap/jackknife replications converge, taking longer compute . Important difference: replications fail converge, sandwich includes failed iterations variance calculation, vcov() implementation uses successful replications. latter statistically appropriate. therefore strongly recommend using native vcov() methods provided mlmodels bootstrap jackknife variance-covariance matrices.

title: Vuong's Test for Non-Nested Models — vuongtest
text: Vuong's test compares two non-nested models testing null hypothesis two models equally close true data generating process. test statistic based difference per-observation log-likelihood contributions two models. positive significant value favors object_1, negative significant value favors object_2, non-significant value leads "inconclusive" result. models must estimated exactly sample.

title: Vuong's Test for Non-Nested Models — vuongtest
text: Vuong, Q. H. (1989). 'Likelihood Ratio Tests Model Selection Non-Nested Hypotheses.' Econometrica, 57(2), 307-333. doi:10.2307/1912557

title: Vuong's Test for Non-Nested Models — vuongtest
code: # Linear models example (lognormal vs gamma) data(mroz) mroz$incthou <- mroz$faminc / 1000  fit_lognormal <- ml_lm(log(incthou) ~ age + I(age^2) + huswage + educ + unem,                        data = mroz)  fit_gamma <- ml_gamma(incthou ~ age + I(age^2) + huswage + educ + unem,                       data = mroz)   vuongtest(fit_lognormal, fit_gamma) #>  #> Vuong's (1989) Test #> -------------------------------------------------- #>   Model 1: Homoskedastic Lognormal Model  #>   Model 2: Homoskedastic Gamma Model  #> -------------------------------------------------- #>   z-stat:  -1.592 #>   p-value: 0.1114 #> -------------------------------------------------- #>  Inconclusive test: neither model is preferred.  # Count models example  fit_poi <- ml_poisson(docvis ~ private + medicaid + age + I(age^2) + educyr +                           actlim + totchr,                       data = docvis)  fit_nb1 <- ml_negbin(docvis ~ private + medicaid + age + I(age^2) + educyr +                           actlim + totchr,                       data = docvis,                       dispersion = "NB1")  fit_nb2 <- ml_negbin(docvis ~ private + medicaid + age + I(age^2) + educyr +                           actlim + totchr,                       data = docvis)                        # Poisson vs. NB1 vuongtest(fit_poi, fit_nb1) #>  #> Vuong's (1989) Test #> -------------------------------------------------- #>   Model 1: Poisson  #>   Model 2: Homoskedastic Negative Binomial (NB1) Model  #> -------------------------------------------------- #>   z-stat:  -12.512 #>   p-value: 0.0000 #> -------------------------------------------------- #>   Homoskedastic Negative Binomial (NB1) Model seems to be preferred.  # NB1 vs. NB2 vuongtest(fit_nb1, fit_nb2) #>  #> Vuong's (1989) Test #> -------------------------------------------------- #>   Model 1: Homoskedastic Negative Binomial (NB1) Model  #>   Model 2: Homoskedastic Negative Binomial (NB2) Model  #> -------------------------------------------------- #>   z-stat:  3.638 #>   p-value: 0.0003 #> -------------------------------------------------- #>   Homoskedastic Negative Binomial (NB1) Model seems to be preferred.  # Binary models example data(smoke) smoke$smokes <- smoke$cigs > 0  fit_logit <- ml_logit(smokes ~ cigpric + income + age, data = smoke) fit_probit <- ml_probit(smokes ~ cigpric + income + age, data = smoke)  vuongtest(fit_logit, fit_probit) #>  #> Vuong's (1989) Test #> -------------------------------------------------- #>   Model 1: Homoskedastic Binary Logit  #>   Model 2: Homoskedastic Binary Probit  #> -------------------------------------------------- #>   z-stat:  -1.158 #>   p-value: 0.2467 #> -------------------------------------------------- #>  Inconclusive test: neither model is preferred.

code: data(mroz) mroz$incthou <- mroz$faminc / 1000  fit <- ml_lm(incthou ~ age + I(age^2) + huswage + educ + unem,               data = mroz)  # 1. Test single coefficients using indices (default OIM) waldtest(fit, indices = c(2, 5)) #>  #> Wald Test of Linear Restrictions #>  #> Variance type: Original Information Matrix #> --------------------------------------- #> Restrictions: #>   1: value::age = 0 #>   2: value::educ = 0 #> Chisq(2) = 59.026    Pr(>Chisq) = < 1e-8 #> -------------------------------------------- #>   # 2. Test using coefficient names and robust standard errors waldtest(fit, coef_names = c("value::educ", "value::unem"),           vcov.type = "robust") #>  #> Wald Test of Linear Restrictions #>  #> Variance type: Robust #> --------------------------------------- #> Restrictions: #>   1: value::educ = 0 #>   2: value::unem = 0 #> Chisq(2) = 43.248    Pr(>Chisq) = < 1e-8 #> -------------------------------------------- #>            # 3. Test explicit constraints waldtest(fit, coef_names = "value::educ", rhs = 1, vcov.type = "robust") #>  #> Wald Test of Linear Restrictions #>  #> Variance type: Robust #> --------------------------------------- #> Restrictions: #>   1: value::educ = 1 #> Chisq(1) = 0.049    Pr(>Chisq) = 0.8256 #> -------------------------------------------- #>   # 4. Test a linear combination of two coefficients using a restriction matrix # H0: educ + huswage = 3 R <- matrix(c(0, 0, 0, 1, 1, 0, 0), nrow = 1) waldtest(fit, rest_matrix = R, rhs = 3, vcov.type = "boot",           repetitions = 100, seed = 123) #>  #> Wald Test of Linear Restrictions #>  #> Variance type: Bootstrap (100/100 reps. - 100.00% rate) #> --------------------------------------- #> Restrictions: #>   1: value::huswage + value::educ = 3 #> Chisq(1) = 0.209    Pr(>Chisq) = 0.6473 #> -------------------------------------------- #>

title: mlmodels 0.1.0
text: First public release. Provides maximum likelihood estimation Gaussian (linear log-normal), logit, probit, Poisson, negative binomial (NB1 NB2), gamma, beta models. Consistent S3 interface support modeling scale parameters. Multiple variance-covariance estimators (OIM, OPG, robust, cluster-robust, bootstrap, jackknife). Full suite post-estimation tools hypothesis tests. Compatible marginaleffects marginal effects predictions. Comprehensive vignettes covering main model families diagnostics.
